title: Inside The Curious World Of Porcupine Meat
full_text: Inside The Curious World Of Porcupine Meat - Palate Asia Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Now Reading: Inside The Curious World Of Porcupine Meat 1 01 Inside The Curious World Of Porcupine Meat Main // Eat // Drink // Reviews // Insider // At Home // About Us // Download Our Magazine // Q2 2026 // Q1 2026 // Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 // Home Eat Inside The Curious World Of Porcupine Meat Inside The Curious World Of Porcupine Meat Colin Gomez Eat 1 month ago Share The further modern life pulls people away from the land, the stranger older food traditions begin to sound. Porcupine meat is perhaps one of the clearest examples of that distance. It’s no surprise that a country like Malaysia, so richly blessed in its biodiversity, would have a relationship with its wildlife that runs complex and deeply intertwined. For most of us reading this, the jungle occupies a space that doesn’t extend far beyond scenery. It’s something to admire through your car window on the way up to Genting, or perhaps a place to spend a weekend resolving to “disconnect”, before promptly breaking that resolution at the first sniff of a WiFi signal. But for others, especially in older rural communities, the forest occupied a much more practical and significant role than that. It was where herbs came from, where medicine came from, and, occasionally, where dinner came from too. And because of this, Malaysia has long had a culinary relationship with meats that might strike more urban diners as unusual. Wild boar, venison, civet in some places, monitor lizard historically, and yes, porcupine. It’s not exactly the sort of thing you’ll find proudly advertised on a glowing signboard beside your local kopitiam, but it’s certainly not unheard of either. Photography: Kati Fleming | Wikimedia Commons The quills of the porcupine can render it a challenge to prepare Now, in the present day, porcupine meat inhabits a somewhat strange space. You’ll find it more often spoken about than actually encountered. Among certain Orang Asli communities and older generations in rural areas, it once occupied a more familiar role, but modern Malaysia has understandably moved away from a lot of these practices. Conservation laws have tightened considerably, and hunting wildlife today is heavily regulated, with some species of porcupine protected under Malaysian law. But there are pockets where the old ways still carry on, only now they’ve been displaced further towards the fringes of public consciousness. On the occasions it is brought into focus, though, the first question that arises on everybody’s lips is: what does it taste like? If you’re expecting something heretofore beyond the realms of your gustatory comprehension, you might end up a little disappointed. And that is because what surprises most people is, in fact, how strangely familiar it actually tastes. Most descriptions put it somewhere between pork and dark chicken meat, with a slightly gamey edge running through it, though not aggressively so. It’s said to be rich, but not overwhelmingly rich, and depending on the cut and preparation, can swing between firm and meaty to almost gelatinous around the collagen-heavy portions. Photography: Jonathan West | Pexels Porcupines eat a varied, seasonal diet of plant matter, with a strong preference for the inner bark of trees during winter. Its diet can affect its flavour Like most wild game, the cooking method matters quite a bit. Grilling it can leave it somewhat tough if you’re not careful, which is why slower preparations tend to be preferred. Curries, pepper-heavy braises, soups, and rendang-style dishes are all common approaches because they allow the meat time to soften, while also mellowing out some of the gamier notes. And Malaysians, being as we are, don’t exactly shy away from assertive flavours either. So naturally, you’ll find the usual suspects making their appearance. Serai. Kunyit. Ginger. Black pepper. Maybe some cili padi if someone’s feeling particularly vindictive that day. The meat gets simmered slowly, patiently, until everything starts collapsing into itself in the way that all good stews should. Photography: @harrietdeltakitchen | Instagram Dried porcupine meat, ready to cook What’s interesting about porcupine meat, though, isn’t so much the taste itself. Or at least not entirely, anyway. It’s more what its existence says about the relationship between people and the environments they inhabit. You see, what constitutes food has always been shaped by a number of different factors: geography, necessity, access, and inheritance, to name but a few. What one community might see as unusual, another might simply recognise as dinner. With all the advancements that continue to accelerate in their proliferation, that gap is certainl
